CalifOrnia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 3, 1991
Honorable Leo Mccarthy
Lieutenant Governor
state capitol, Suite 1114
Sacramento, CA 9 5814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
our File No. I-90-734
^ear Mr. McCarthy:
This Ietter is in response to your request for advice
-".;i;; 
ihe provi.sion= oi the Political Reform Act (the rrn"grrl '1
/o,lr req'i"st i! general in nature. Accordingly, we treat. your
letter is a request for infor:naI assistance pursuant to the
lrovisions of iegulation 18329.2
OUESTIONS
1. would funds generated by private publication of the
Report of the California Pacific Year 20oo Task Force be
considered funds due to the State of California, and if so should
inoi. f""a. be applied to the funds of the comnission for Economic
Development? Tt not, what would be the appropriate disposition of
those funds?
2. Would private pubtication of the report conflict with any
statutory or regutatory authority of the Fair Political Practices
Cornmiss ion?
1 co,r".n*"nt code Seltions 81ooo-91015. ALI statutory references
are to the Governnent code unless otherwise indicated. 
commission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulatj'ons. Section
18ooo, ets seq. A11 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
inmunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(section 83114; Regulation 18329 (c) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 3 22-5660


Our Fi■e No. I-90-734
Page 2
CONCLUS I ONS
1. The Fair Political Practices Cornmission does not have the
authority to advise you on whether funds generated by publication
of the report would have to be donated to the State of California
or to any agency of the State. The commission's jurisdiction is
linited Lo tne provisions of the Act. There are no provisions in
the Act which woutd affect the disposition of funds raised through
the k j.nd of publishing activity described in your letter. 
we
suggest that you refer your questions on this subject to the
ofiice of the Attorney General for clarification.
2. Private publication of the rePort witl not conflict with
the Act or any regulation promulgated thereunder. However, the
provisions of the Act may prohibit you from receiving any
tompensation in the form of roya).ties if the rnaterial's prepared
f or publicat j,on constitute an 'rarticle publ'ished. rl
FACTS
You are the statutory chairman of the California Commission
tor Economic Development (cED), a bipartisan organization created
:o advocate issues ielating to california's econonic strength and
-Frf.r-nance. The CED was established in 1971 and authorized under
-.:ction 14999, et seq. The CED is authorized to accept both
government and Private f undi'ng.
In February of 1989, as chair of the cED, you were invited to
testify at Cal.iiornia field hearj.ngs held by the U' S' senate
roreioir ReLations connittee's Subcornnittee on East Asian and
pacific Affairs.3 
Broadly favorable response to the hearings led
you to join the subconmittee Chairman, U. s. Senator Alan
transtoi, and Representative MeI Levine in forming a task force of
distingui.shed californians to address california's 
unique
intereits in the Pacific Rim. The task force came to be known as
the california-Pacific 
Year 2ooo Task Force (the "Task Forcerr).
The task force is comPosed of 34 california Ieaders in diverse
areas including exporting, manufacturing, academia and the Asian
Anerican communLty. Membership on the task force also includes
you as its chairmln and one commissioner from the cED. The task
force is not a governmental. entj.ty.
In August, 1990, the task force released its report entitled
california and the Pacif i.c in the New centurv: A Strateqic Plan
for Succes;. one of the co-chairs of the task force has expressed
an interest in offering the f j.na1 report of the task force for
private pubJ.ication in Japan. The publishers $/ould, in alL
tikelj.hood, offer an advance and royalty Pa)rments.
3 These facts
Report of the
contain information
ca lif ornia Comrnissionextracted from the 1989 Annual
for Economic DeveloPnent.


Our File No. I-90-734
Page 3
ANALYS I S
Section 83114 of the Act pennits the commission to render
written advice lrith respect to a person's duties under the Act.
The jurisdiction of the Comrnission is, houever, linited to the
provisions of the Act. There is no provision in the Act related
to the disposition of funds received as a result of the private
publication of a report such as the Report of the california-
Pacific Year 2ooo Task Force. we suqgest that you contact the
office of the Attorney General for advice regardinq the
appropriateness of such a publication and the disposition of funds
glir".itea fron the publication of the task force report. 
If it is
ieternined that you would be the recipient of any of the funds,
however, pertinent provisions of the Act woutd apply as discussed
belolr.
You have requested our advice to deterrnine whether private
publication of the task force report $rouId conflict uith any of
the statutory or regulatory authority adninistered by the
Commission. If you will not receive any Payments as a result of
the publication of the report, its publication would not violate
:-ne provisions of the Act. However, certain newly-enacted
provisions of the Act may be applicable to the publication if you
l:uld be the recipient of any of the funds generated from the
publ ication.
Effective January 1, 199L, the Legislature enacted a series
of statutes that ban ieceipt of honoraria by Public officials' 
By
virtue of your position as Lieutenant Governor for the state of
California, you are a public officiaL. 
(Sections 82048 and 82053')
section 895o2 provides that consideration for an 'rarticLe
publj.shed" is an honorarium. Honoraria are prohibited under the
statutory provisions which states as foLlows:
(a) No elected state officer may accept an
honorariun.
(b) rrHonorariunrr means, except as provided in
subdivision (c), any paynent made in consideration
for any speech gi.ven, article published, or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meaI, or Iike
gathering.
(c) rrHonorariunrr does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection $/ith the individual's
business or practice of a bona fide business,
trade, or profession, such as teaching, practicing
Iaw, medicine, insurance, real estate, banking, or
buitding contracting, unless the sole or
predoninant activity of the business, trade, or
profession is rnaking speeches. The conrnission


shaII adopt regulations to iruplenent this
subdivision.
Section 89502.
Thus, as an elected state officer you nay not accept a payment for
an article published. The prohibition applies regardless of
hrhether the article relates to your officj.al duties'
Section 89502 (c) provides that the term rrhonorariumrr does not
include earned income tor personal services which are custonarily
lroviaea in connection witl the official,s 
bona fide business or
irade. 
what constitutes a rrbona f iderr business is not
specificaffy defined in the statute. 
Currently, -:taff is advising
tirat a busii:ess would be treated as rrbona fide[ if the owner of
the business maintained books and records of accounting for the
business and tax records filed in connection with the business for
the tr./o calendar years preceding the paynent in question' 
(Greene
Advice Letter, No. f-gf-za2; Esqro Advice Letter, No' I-91-339')
Hor.,/ever, you have not indicated in your request for advice that
you are eirgaged in any busj'ness and thus this exception is
j- nappl icable to Your facts.
on the other hand, receipt of compensation for published
-^cks and screenplays is not prohibited. This is so because the
.;rohibition on honoraria specifies only articles, not books' An
;'irii"fe 
published,' would appear to inilude manuscripts published
or producLd for pubJ.ication in perj.odicals, iournals, ne$/spapers,
newiletters, magazines, pamphlets, or similar publications'
(creene, supra,' Esoro. supra.){ Therefore, if the proposed
,i"ff i"uii"n it .,ot "n "article published" as defined above, the
ict would not prohibit you, as ln el'ected state official, 
from
accepting compensation for its publication.
You have not provided us r.rith sufficient information to
deternine whether the report prepared by the task force wiIl be
published in the form of a uook or whether it will be published as
an article. 
Additionally, it appears fron your request for advice
that you do not intend to receivL payments as a result of this
publilation. 
However, should you decide to accept. royaLties when
the materials are published, you nust first determine whether the
materials constitute an article or a book. As stated above,
rece j.pt of paynents for publication of an article is prohibited'
conveise 1y, ' rece j.pt of piyments generated from the publication of
a book is- not proirj.litea under the act. 
Should you deternine that
the report has been published in the forn of a book, you may
receivl payments genLrated frorn its publication. 
Any royalties

Page 4

that will clarify the
(prOpOSed Regulation
now in the process of
meaning of the term
■8931.2.)
adopting a regulation
I'article publ ished. rl


Our File No. I-9 0-7 3 4
Page 5
you may receive for your participation in the production of the
book would be income subject to reporting in your Commission For:n
721 Statements of Econonic Interests.
we trust this letter adeguately responds to your inquiry.
should you have any further questJ.ons regarding this natter, do
not hesitate to call ne at (916) 322-590L.'
S incere ly ,
Scott Hall.abrin
Counsel, Legal Division

many Ia$/ libraries.

Comrnission advice letters rnay be obtained fron the Conmission at a
cost of 10C per page.
